intro: These stories did not drift down to us on the wind. They survived two ways: in the archives that kept the tablets and scrolls, and in the historians and sages who set them down as history. Here are both — the places, and the people — and where you can still find them.

witnesses.name: Ashurbanipal's Library, Nineveh
witnesses.preserved: Gilgamesh & the Flood tablet, Enuma Elish, Atrahasis, thousands more
witnesses.story: The oldest surviving library on earth — some 30,000 cuneiform tablets, gathered by an Assyrian king who deliberately collected the knowledge of the world, including texts said to come from before the Flood. Here, in 1872, George Smith found and read the Babylonian flood account, stunning Victorian England. Excavated from the mound across the river from modern Mosul; parts of Nineveh were damaged by ISIS in 2015.

witnesses.name: Qumran — the Dead Sea Scrolls
witnesses.preserved: Oldest Hebrew Bible texts; 1 Enoch; the Book of Giants; Jubilees; the Genesis Apocryphon
witnesses.story: In 1947 a shepherd threw a stone into a cave and heard pottery break. The jars held some 900 manuscripts, hidden for nearly two thousand years — among them the oldest copies of the Hebrew scriptures by a thousand years, and the lost books of Enoch and the giants that this storybook leans on.

witnesses.name: Saqqara & the Pyramid Texts
witnesses.preserved: The Pyramid Texts — the world's oldest religious writings — then the Coffin Texts and Book of the Dead
witnesses.story: Carved inside the burial chambers of Old Kingdom pharaohs, the Pyramid Texts are older than the Bible, older than Gilgamesh in writing — Egypt's account of the primeval waters of Nun, the rising of the first land, and the death and resurrection of Osiris.

witnesses.name: Nippur & the Sumerian tablets
witnesses.preserved: The Sumerian King List, the Eridu Genesis flood tablet, most Sumerian literature
witnesses.story: The scribal heart of Sumer. Its tablet-houses gave us the oldest written flood account (Ziusudra), the King List that splits history at the Flood, and the descent of Inanna — now gathered in Oxford's Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature.

witnesses.name: Ras Shamra (Ugarit)
witnesses.preserved: The Baal Cycle — Lotan/Leviathan, the storm-god, the dying-and-rising Baal
witnesses.story: A farmer's plough struck a tomb in 1928, opening a lost city and a forgotten alphabet. Its tablets are the scriptures of Israel's Canaanite neighbors — and they tell the dragon-fight and the dying-and-rising god in the very same language family as the Hebrew Bible.

witnesses.name: Berossus & Manetho (the Greek copyists)
witnesses.preserved: Lost Babylonian and Egyptian priestly histories, quoted by later writers
witnesses.story: A Babylonian priest (Berossus) and an Egyptian priest (Manetho) wrote their nations' deep histories in Greek — the pre-flood kings, the Flood, the dynasties. Their books are lost, but survive in fragments because Greek and Roman authors quoted them, passing the witness on.

witnesses.name: Flavius Josephus
witnesses.preserved: Antiquities of the Jews — the Flood, the giants, Nimrod's tower; quotes lost historians
witnesses.story: A Jewish priest-historian writing for Rome, Josephus laid down the deep history of his people as history — and to back it he quoted Babylonian and Greek historians whose books are now lost, including Berossus's and Nicolaus's reports that the ark's remains were still shown on a mountain in Armenia.

witnesses.name: Herodotus
witnesses.preserved: The Histories — Egypt's and Babylon's antiquity, the tower of Bel, the phoenix
witnesses.story: The 'Father of History.' He travelled to Egypt and Babylon, questioned their priests, and measured their monuments himself — recording the eight-staged tower of Bel and the vast chronology the Egyptian priests claimed for their land.

witnesses.name: Hermes Trismegistus
witnesses.preserved: The Corpus Hermeticum & the Emerald Tablet — Egyptian creation, astronomy, 'as above, so below'
witnesses.story: 'Thrice-great Hermes,' identified with the Egyptian god of writing Thoth: a body of Greco-Egyptian wisdom literature (c. 100–300 CE) that preserves a creation narrative echoing Genesis — a Word and a Light over the watery dark. The Corpus Hermeticum was written centuries after the Septuagint made Genesis available in Greek in Egypt; it is not an independent witness, but it shows the Genesis creation narrative was still being read and adapted in the Greco-Egyptian world a millennium after it was written.

witnesses.name: The monasteries of Ethiopia (the Ge'ez canon)
witnesses.era: manuscripts 14th c. CE and later; texts translated into Ge'ez c. 5th–6th c. CE
witnesses.preserved: The only complete Book of Enoch (1 Enoch) on earth; the complete Book of Jubilees
witnesses.story: When the rest of the world let the Book of Enoch fall out of copying, the Ethiopian church kept it in its Bible — and its monasteries kept copying it, century after century. In 1773 the Scottish traveler James Bruce carried three manuscripts back to Europe, restoring the full book to the world. When the Dead Sea Scrolls were opened almost two centuries later, Aramaic fragments of Enoch matched the Ethiopian text — the monks had been faithfully transmitting a genuinely ancient book all along.

witnesses.name: Codex Regius — the Poetic Edda's one manuscript
witnesses.preserved: Völuspá, the Norse creation and doom of the gods, and most of the old mythological poems
witnesses.story: Nearly everything this book quotes from the Norse — Ginnungagap, the flood of Ymir's blood, Fimbulwinter, Thor and the serpent — survives principally because one unassuming vellum book was kept in an Icelandic farmhouse until a bishop acquired it in 1643. No other manuscript preserves most of these poems. The old memory of the North hung, for centuries, on a single object the size of a paperback.

witnesses.name: The Ximénez manuscript (Popol Vuh)
witnesses.era: copied c. 1701–03, from a 1550s K'iche' original now lost
witnesses.preserved: The Popol Vuh — the Maya creation from the calm sea, the flood of the wooden people
witnesses.story: A K'iche' noble family wrote their nation's sacred book in alphabetic script in the 1550s, working from the old painted books the conquest was burning. Around 1701 the Dominican friar Francisco Ximénez, trusted enough to be shown it, copied it out with a parallel Spanish translation — and his copy is the only one that survives. The New World's most complete ancient scripture exists today because of one act of trust between a Maya town and its priest.
